metadata.title: Why the Average That Clears the Model Hides the Patient It Harms

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to feel that fifteen-to-one drowning effect with my own hands. I'm going to take a synthetic cohort of 9,500 well-calibrated adults and 95 badly-calibrated elderly patients and watch the global ECE stay clean. Help me: (1) write the formula for size-weighted ECE and show algebraically how the elderly term nearly vanishes; (2) compute per-subgroup ECE next to the aggregate for my numbers; (3) suggest a minimum subgroup-size threshold below which I should refuse to trust an aggregate calibration score.

props.command: I want to feel that fifteen-to-one drowning effect with my own hands. beats.narration_text: The average clears the model. The patient pays.

card.copy:
Why the Average That Clears the Model
Hides the Patient It Harms

beats.narration_text: A hospital AI model passes calibration review. Global ECE: low. Looks fine. Then someone checks the elderly subgroup separately. That subgroup's calibration is more than twice as bad. The aggregate number was correct. The aggregate number was hiding a real failure — because the elderly subgroup was small enough to wash out in the average.
How can a calibration score be correct — and simultaneously be hiding a failure that affects real patients?

props.headline: How can a calibration score be correct — and simultaneously be…

card.copy:
How can a calibration score be correct
and hide a real failure at the same time?

beats.narration_text: Aggregate calibration metrics are averages. Averages are size-weighted. A large subgroup with good calibration drowns out a small subgroup with bad calibration. The global ECE can be low — genuinely — while a specific group is catastrophically miscalibrated.

Think of it on the reliability diagram. The large group's curve lies on the diagonal. The small group's curve is far off. Merge the two groups into one aggregate and the small group's deviation dissolves — absorbed by the majority it was outnumbered by.
Small enough to wash out. Large enough to harm.

props.headline: Small enough to wash out. Large enough to harm.

beats.image_description: A medical chart or electronic health record on a monitor in a hospital room, with rows of data and one row highlighted in red — the visual of a single patient inside an aggregate statistic

beats.narration_text: This is the structural problem. The aggregate metric is computed across all patients. If the high-risk elderly subgroup — ninety-five cases — is outnumbered fifteen to one by the adult majority, its miscalibration contributes almost nothing to the global ECE. The number stays clean. The failure stays invisible.

The single most important limitation of aggregate calibration metrics is that they are aggregate. A system with low global calibration error can be catastrophically miscalibrated for a specific subgroup if that subgroup is small enough to be washed out in the overall average.

The fix is not a better formula — it is computation on a different unit. Run a separate reliability diagram and separate calibration error for each named subgroup. At minimum: demographic subgroups, severity strata, any group you have reason to believe differs from the training distribution.
The aggregate metric is evidence the model is calibrated on average. It is not evidence the model is calibrated for the patient in front of you.

If your maximum subgroup calibration error is substantially higher than your global calibration error, the aggregate metric is concealing a real problem. Report both. If the maximum subgroup error is high, the global number is not your headline — it is your cover story.

An insurance pricing model passes calibration review. Overall ECE: 0.03 — well under the 0.05 threshold. But the elderly rural applicant subgroup — eight percent of all applicants — has ECE 0.21. Their risk is being systematically underestimated. Claims in that subgroup run eighteen percent over prediction. The aggregate metric was accurate. It was hiding the failure in the eight percent.

Aggregate calibration averages across subgroups. Small subgroups wash out. The global number can be clean while the failure is real. Compute subgroup metrics separately — not as an optional check, but as the only way to know whether the model is calibrated for the patient in front of you.

props.headline: Aggregate calibration averages across subgroups. Small subgroups wash out. The global…

card.copy:
The average clears the model.
The subgroup pays.
card.sub: Aggregate calibration is not evidence of subgroup calibration.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The fix is not a better formula — it is computation on a different unit. Run a separate reliability diagram and separate calibration error for each named subgroup. At minimum: demographic subgroups, severity strata, any group you have reason to believe differs from the training distribution. The aggregate metric is evidence the model is calibrated on average. It is not evidence the model is calibrated for the patient in front of you.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
